symbols: radiant light emanating behind Mary — divine glory surrounding the Mother of God
putti supporting Mary and child — the heavenly host attending the incarnate God
dark mantle with red lining — Mary's sorrow and the blood of the Passion foreshadowed
child clinging to mother — the genuine humanity of the divine Son
shadowy faces in the background — angelic presences in the celestial realm
description: A Mannerist composition showing a young woman with an elongated neck and refined features, wearing a dark blue-green mantle with red lining. She holds a red-haired child who clings to her chest. Below and around them, four or five curly-haired putti cluster together, their plump bodies intertwined. Rays of light emanate outward from behind the woman's head. In the shadowy background, partially obscured faces or figures are dimly visible. The palette is dominated by dark greens, deep reds, and warm flesh tones.
theological_description: Rosso Fiorentino's Mannerist Madonna and Child presents the Virgin and the Christ child in a celestial setting, surrounded by attending putti who represent the angelic host. The radiance behind Mary connects to the vision in Revelation 12:1 of 'a woman clothed with the sun,' which has been traditionally interpreted as both Israel and Mary. The child's act of clinging to his mother emphasizes the full humanity of Christ — the eternal Word who 'became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14) experienced genuine human dependency and maternal love. The putti, derived from the biblical cherubim, recall the heavenly attendants who surround God's throne (Isaiah 6:1-3) and who now attend the incarnate God in his mother's arms. Paul's letter to the Philippians describes Christ as one who, 'being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage; rather, he made himself nothing' (Philippians 2:6-7). Here the King of the universe is a child held in a woman's arms — the paradox of the Incarnation rendered in paint.

scripture_refs.reason: The Word made flesh — the theological foundation of every Madonna and Child image
The woman clothed with the sun, traditionally associated with Mary, echoed in the radiance behind her
Christ's self-emptying and taking human form — the divine child dependent on a human mother
The angelic hosts attending the divine presence, represented by the putti surrounding Christ
